title: Combined Metagenomic / Metatranscriptomic Pipeline for Host-associated Microbiomes
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"58cpr","text":"Overview:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"a2d6r","text":"1. Filter DNA reads by quality and host (e.g. for mouse gut bacteria we would filter low-quality reads AND reads that belong to mice)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4iqsm","text":"2. Align DNA reads (fastq, fasta) to Patric database (bacteria genomes) with taxoner (a bowtie2-based linux program) \u003e\u003e Get bacterial genomes","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":37,"length":6},{"key":1,"offset":77,"length":7}],"data":[]},{"key":"635b9","text":"3. Filter out low-quality alignments \u003e\u003e Bacterial genomes","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3lk18","text":"4. Filter RNA reads by quality","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"e6202","text":"5. Align RNA reads to bacterial genomes from step 3 \u003e\u003e RNA counts of genes","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"cp052","text":"6. Get pathway information \u003e\u003e RNA counts per KEGG pathway","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6lcvp","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"c61jq","text":"If you have treatment groups, we will be able to see changes in bacterial abundance as well as changes to RNA counts","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"780A462FEB2D4F64A63B5599D8A01880","url":"https://www.patricbrc.org/"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"ED2FEAB4D8F04E47A57BA720C846FEB6","url":"https://github.com/r78v10a07/taxoner"}}}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"88fmf","text":"Unless you have a small set of reads and/or genomes, it is recommended that the pipeline be run on university supercomputers.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Get sample data from github (requires git to be installed on your system). git clone https://github.com/hurwitzlab/mg-sample-data.git This can be downloaded to your shared supercomputers (job scheduler managed) or your own laptop. It's small enough data (< 10mb) that it should be trivial to run on most systems.
Get recipe for singularity image containing fastqc and solexaqa: (This must be downloaded to a system where you have sudo privileges) git clone https://github.com/hurwitzlab/singularity-fastqc.git

From within /singularity-fastqc run ./create_and_bootstrap.sh (see example). This should create a 'fastqc.img'. cd singularity-fastqc/ && ./create_and_bootstrap.sh Make sure there are no errors! E.g. "ABORT: Aborting with RETVAL=255" http://null
sftp or scp the created "fastqc.img" to your shared supercomputer directory OR put it in your "/bin" directory (or other dir in your PATH) Example:

Generate quality reports with fastqc (optional). Example script is mg-sample-data/scripts/00-fastqc-reports.shThis script is meant to be submitted to a PBS job scheduler by issuing the command Example script is mg-sample-data/scripts/00-fastqc-reports.sh This script is meant to be submitted to a PBS job scheduler by issuing the command "./00-fastqc-reports.sh" but can be edited to run with other job schedulers (must also edit ./workers/fastqc.sh). If you want to manually generate fastqc reports, the script you want is in 'scripts/workers/fastqc.sh' This is true for most other steps.

Run trim_galore (a wrapper script for cutadapt) to cut off low-quality bases and adapters (default is to cut off bases until the probability that the base call was correct is 99% or greater) Example script is mg-sample-data/scripts/01-trim-galore.shThis script is meant to be submitted to a PBS job array scheduler by running the command Example script is mg-sample-data/scripts/01-trim-galore.sh This script is meant to be submitted to a PBS job array scheduler by running the command "./01-trim-galore.sh" but can be edited to run with other job schedulers (must also edit "./workers/trimgalore.sh")
Compare quality reports between steps 5 and 6. If you don't see much improvement, consider re-running step 6 with different parameters.

Get the github repos for building the images git clone https://github.com/hurwitzlab/singularity-tuxedo.gitgit clone https://github.com/hurwitzlab/singularity-taxoner.git git clone https://github.com/hurwitzlab/singularity-tuxedo.git git clone https://github.com/hurwitzlab/singularity-taxoner.git

From within /singularity-tuxedo (Tuxedo is a suite of tools that contains bowtie and cuffdiff see http://software.broadinstitute.org/cancer/software/genepattern/rna-seq-analysis#tuxedo) run ./create_and_bootstrap.sh (see example). This should create a 'bowcuff.img'. From within /singularity-taxoner run ./create_and_bootstrap.sh. This should create a 'taxoner.img' Make sure there are no errors! E.g. "ABORT: Aborting with RETVAL=255" http://null cd singularity-tuxedo/ && ./create_and_bootstrap.sh
sftp or scp the created 'bowcuff.img' and 'taxoner.img' to your shared supercomputer directory OR put it in your '/bin' directory (or other dir in your PATH) Example:

Run 02-taxadb-prep.pbs to prepare the lineage file and genome fasta's for bowtie2 indexing Example script is mg-sample-data/scripts/02-taxadb-prep.pbsThis script is meant to be submitted to a PBS job scheduler by issuing the command Example script is mg-sample-data/scripts/02-taxadb-prep.pbs This script is meant to be submitted to a PBS job scheduler by issuing the command "qsub 02-taxadb-prep.pbs" but can be edited to run with other job schedulers.

Make Bowtie2 indices of the split multi-genome fasta files. The example script will launch bowtie2 to run index building on two computers in parallel. This is why you split the giant fasta into files of ~4 Gb each. So if you had 40 gb worth of genomes in one big file, you could have 10 computers doing the bowtie2 indexing at once. Example script is mg-sample-data/scripts/03-bowtie2-build.shThis script is meant to be submitted to a PBS job scheduler by issuing the command Example script is mg-sample-data/scripts/03-bowtie2-build.sh This script is meant to be submitted to a PBS job scheduler by issuing the command "./03-bowtie2-build.sh" but can be edited to run with other job schedulers (must also edit ./workers/bowtie2-build.sh).
Edit config.sh with your favorite text editor to set variables (directories mainly) Example:

Get the singularity container for centrifuge a classifier program for bacterial/viral species (https://ccb.jhu.edu/software/centrifuge/) If doing this step for the species identification you can optionally skip QC and definitely skip Bowtie2 Prep git clone https://github.com/scottdaniel/singularity-centrifuge.git
Build the singularity container as before cd singularity-centrifuge && make img
We need to build a couple more singularity images for this section: singularity-taxoner and singularity-tuxedo (named after the tuxedo suite of tools: bowtie2, cufflinks, etc.)

Run taxoner for the DNA reads to determine species composition Example script is mg-sample-data/scripts/04-taxoner.shThis script is meant to be submitted to a PBS job scheduler by issuing the command Example script is mg-sample-data/scripts/04-taxoner.sh This script is meant to be submitted to a PBS job scheduler by issuing the command "./04-taxoner.sh" but can be edited to run with other job schedulers (must also edit ./workers/runTaxoner.sh).
creator: Scott Daniel
